bibliographic_data.title: Explaining Non-monotonic Normative Reasoning using Argumentation Theory with Deontic Logic
bibliographic_data.abstract: In our previous research, we provided a reasoning system (called LeSAC) based on argumentation theory to provide legal support to designers during the design process. Building on this, this paper explores how to provide designers with effective explanations for their legally relevant design decisions. We extend the previous system for providing explanations by specifying norms and the key legal or ethical principles for justifying actions in normative contexts. Considering that first-order logic has strong expressive power, in the current paper we adopt a first-order deontic logic system with deontic operators and preferences. We illustrate the advantages and necessity of introducing deontic logic and designing explanations under LeSAC by modelling two cases in the context of autonomous driving.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper introduces a first-order deontic logic system to enhance the LeSAC reasoning framework for providing legally relevant design explanations in normative contexts, particularly in autonomous driving scenarios.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors extend the previous LeSAC system by incorporating deontic logic operators and preferences. They model two cases in autonomous driving to illustrate the advantages of this approach.
outcomes: Demonstrated the necessity of using deontic logic for explaining design decisions in normative contexts, especially in scenarios involving conflicting values.
Proved that a well-defined LeSAC can satisfy rationality postulates for rule-based argumentation frameworks.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, the authors build upon their previous research on providing legal support to designers through the LeSAC reasoning system. They introduce deontic logic into the framework to address issues of 'deontological collapse' and provide clearer explanations for design decisions in normative contexts, particularly in autonomous driving scenarios. The paper models two cases to illustrate these points and proves that a well-defined LeSAC can meet rationality postulates.
